480:
Sara Duterte filed a 15-page "Answer Ad Cautelam" to the impeachment complaint. This is an important document. Let me walk through what she said, what she didn't say, and what the April 8 trial date actually means.

HOW THE "ANSWER" DODGES THE CASE
The phrase "ad cautelam" means "as a precaution"—she's filing this response without conceding that the Senate has jurisdiction over her impeachment. This is a legal hedge. By using this framing, she preserves her right to argue later that the whole proceeding is invalid, even while responding to the substance.

THE "DOUBLE STANDARD" CARD
Sara's answer argues that the House resolution was politically motivated and that the impeachment was rushed without proper procedure. She invokes what her legal team calls a double standard—claiming she's being treated differently from other officials who faced similar accusations.

FOLLOW THE MONEY
The core financial question is about the P125 million in confidential funds allocated to her office. Her answer doesn't deny that the funds existed or that they were spent. Her defense centers on the claim that the expenditures were lawful and that confidential fund procedures were followed.

"MARY GRACE PIATTOS" AND "KOKOY VILLAMIN"
Two names appear in the testimony record that have become shorthand for the alleged misuse. Sara's answer addresses the testimony of witnesses who claimed to have received cash from these intermediaries, but disputes the characterization of those transactions.

RAMIL MADRIAGA AND THE BAGS OF CASH
Ramil Madriaga, a former aide, gave testimony about cash distributions allegedly traced back to Sara's confidential funds. Her answer challenges his credibility and the chain of custody of the alleged evidence.

THE ASSASSINATION LINE
Perhaps the most widely reported part of her earlier statements was the allegation that she had planned for the assassination of President Marcos, First Lady Liza Araneta-Marcos, and House Speaker Martin Romualdez if she were killed. Her legal filing walks back or contextualizes this statement.

THE ONE-YEAR BAR AND THE TECHNICAL ESCAPE
Under the Constitution, an official can only be impeached once per year. Sara's legal team argues that the timeline of the House proceedings may have violated this bar, which could provide grounds for dismissal on procedural grounds.

WHO'S STILL WITH HER
Despite the impeachment, Sara still has political allies, particularly in Mindanao and among Duterte loyalists. Her legal team includes prominent lawyers who have handled high-profile political cases.

THE "FISHING EXPEDITION" LINE
Sara's answer repeatedly uses the phrase "fishing expedition" to describe the Senate proceedings—arguing that prosecutors are looking for crimes to charge her with rather than investigating a specific, well-defined accusation.

WHAT APRIL 8 IS REALLY ABOUT
April 8 is set as a preliminary conference date, not the full trial. The real question is whether the Senate impeachment court proceeds to full trial, what evidence gets admitted, and whether Sara shows up at all.

ALL THE THINGS SHE DIDN'T DENY
Here is where the document becomes most revealing. Her answer does not directly deny: that P125 million in confidential funds was disbursed; that cash was given to individuals identified in testimony; that intermediaries named in testimony existed and worked with her office. She challenges the characterization and legality of those acts—but not the basic facts.

481:
It took me several days to research this article—if you scroll down to the source list, you'll understand why. Listening to Lacson and Marcoleta argue about the Kalayaan Group of Islands, I realized something unsettling: both of them were wrong in important ways—but for different reasons. So this blog isn't a final verdict or a patriotic speech. It's my attempt, as one Filipino reader, to actually trace what the documents say.

We need to say this upfront, kasi mahalaga: the Kalayaan Island Group (KIG) is ours to defend. Not because Tomas Cloma "discovered" empty islands in 1956, but because for decades the Philippine state has administered, garrisoned, and built community in those islands.

This piece is a byproduct of research, caffeine, and curiosity. I'm not a historian, just a student of history trying to trace where Kalayaan really came from based on primary and secondary documents. If I missed something or read a source poorly, I'm happy to be corrected.

As I went deeper into the archives and rewatched the hearings, one thing kept bothering me: the origin story we keep telling is so weak, it makes the real legal grounds easier to attack. The danger is that the fake story is so weak, it makes the real legal grounds easier to attack.

If you stay with me, we'll walk through three things: how the discovery myth grew, what the documents actually show, and why Marcoleta—despite being right about one narrow historical point—is using that truth in a way that weakens our position.

The latest proof that our own narrative is cracking came in a February 2026 hearing, when the Senate turned into something like a history classroom.

On one side, Panfilo Lacson was defending the familiar story many Filipinos grew up with: Tomas Cloma discovered the Spratlys because they were res nullius—the thing belongs to no one. On the other side, Rodante Marcoleta was calling that story a distortion. He argued that by 1956, the Spratlys were already discovered, mapped, claimed, and occupied by Vietnam and others. He kept returning to one idea: our modern claim stands on effective control and state administration—not Cloma.

If you follow Philippine politics, it's jarring to hear that from someone like Marcoleta. I've criticized him sharply in other posts. But on this specific point, the historical record largely supports what he said.

WHY THE DISCOVERY STORY NEVER REALLY WORKED

The discovery story sounds simple. You imagine an empty sea, a brave Filipino pioneer, a flag on an island. But under international law, that story was never legally sufficient. According to my research, by the early 20th century—especially after cases like Island of Palmas—international law had already moved past the idea that simply finding a place gives you permanent ownership of it. Under modern doctrine, discovery only gives what Max Huber in the Island of Palmas case called an inchoate title—a seed of a claim that needs to be developed through actual occupation and administration. Planting a flag or writing a note to "the whole world" is not enough; you need administration, governance, and a continuous state presence. Huber also rejected the idea that islands belong to whoever is closest, so "malapit sa Palawan" by itself doesn't give the Philippines title.

That's already a problem for the way we talk about Cloma. He was a private citizen, not a state. Even if he had stumbled on something new, discovery alone would not establish perfect title, and he was not representing the Philippine government.

The 2016 South China Sea arbitral award doesn't rescue the myth either. The tribunal was clear that it had no jurisdiction to rule on territorial sovereignty; it dealt with maritime zones under UNCLOS, not who owns what island. When it mentions Cloma at all, it's as a businessman trying to devise a commercial scheme who never acquired sovereignty.

So even before we open the archives, the legal scaffolding of the "discovery and donation" story looks fragile.

THE LONG HISTORY BEFORE CLOMA EVEN ARRIVED

Once you look at the timeline, the idea that the Spratlys were "undiscovered" in 1956 collapses fast. Long before Cloma was born, regional powers already treated these waters as part of their administrative and strategic geography.

Vietnamese chronicles under the Nguyen dynasty describe offshore administration, with Emperor Gia Long establishing administrative control over offshore islands in the early 1800s. By 1833, Emperor Minh Mang had officials planting trees on the islands for navigation safety. In 1837, Jean-Louis Taberd published a Note on the Geography of Cochinchina naming and mapping Paracel-area islands.

Those records suggest that regional states weren't guessing where these features were; they were administering them. The very word "Spratly" comes from British activity. In 1843, Captain Richard Spratly recorded features while passing through. By 1877 and 1889, British authorities in Labuan were formally granting guano leases over specific features.

France then moved from maps to formal annexation. In 1930 it claimed sovereignty over the Spratly Island and neighboring features. The French Navy built infrastructure, including a weather station on Itu Aba in 1938. Japan did not treat the area as empty either. In 1939 it invaded the Spratlys, drove out the French garrison, and administered the islands as part of its wartime empire.

When Japan surrendered in 1945, the Allies ordered Japanese forces in the area to surrender to the Republic of China (ROC). France did not simply walk away in silence after the war. In October 1946, it sent the battleship Chevreuil to re-establish a presence. At the same time, the newly formed People's Republic of China (PRC) adopted the nine-dash line.

The 1951 San Francisco Peace Treaty added legal fog: Article 2(f) made Japan renounce the Spratlys without naming a beneficiary. That omission created room for confusion. It did not erase Vietnamese records, British leases, French annexation, or Japanese administration. By the time Cloma's ship left Manila, the sea was already crowded with flags, treaties, and bases.

CLOMA'S GAMBIT AND THE WORLD'S ANSWER

Tomas Cloma was not a cartoon villain. He was a Boholano lawyer, a fishing magnate, and founder of PMI Colleges. He wanted to expand his fishing operations, build a cannery, and tap guano deposits.

On March 1, 1956, he hosted a farewell dinner in Manila. On March 15, the training ship PMI-IV, commanded by his brother Filemon and crewed by forty men, reached the area. Four days later, Cloma issued his "Notice to the Whole World", declaring absolute ownership over the islands he called "Freedomland."

By July 6, Cloma had gone further, declaring a separate government for Freedomland with its capital at Flat Island. If the area had genuinely been res nullius, this might have become a strange footnote in maritime history. Instead, the reaction was immediate and hostile. Taiwan's ROC government summoned the Philippine ambassador to demand explanations, then in June 1956 dispatched a naval task force to re-garrison Itu Aba (Taiping Island). When Cloma stole an ROC flag from Itu Aba, he ended up having to apologize and return it. The PRC's foreign ministry issued a statement on May 29, 1956, reaffirming China's "indisputable" sovereignty. France sent a formal note on June 7. South Vietnam, on June 1, 1956, issued its own communique reasserting historical sovereignty.

The speed, anger, and military moves tell you everything you need to know. The world did not see an empty sea that a Filipino adventurer had just discovered. They saw a contested archipelago where someone was trying to exploit a legal opening.

HOW THE PHILIPPINE STATE QUIETLY REWROTE THE STORY

Inside Malacañang, Cloma's stunt was not viewed as a patriotic masterstroke. Ramon Magsaysay's administration initially saw it as a potential trigger for conflict with Taiwan and Vietnam. Military and foreign policy advisers opposed adopting his claim. Carlos P. Garcia, as Foreign Secretary, had to face furious embassies and formally state that Cloma was acting as a private citizen.

To protect Philippine interests without endorsing the res nullius claim, Garcia floated a trusteeship concept. That stance is crucial: it shows the state did not, at that time, treat Cloma's 'discovery' as a legally sound foundation.

Over the next decade, that tone changed. By 1968, the Armed Forces of the Philippines were already moving to occupy several unoccupied features. Then Marcos did what Marcos usually did: centralized and coerced. In 1974, his government had Cloma arrested. While in detention, Cloma was pressured into signing a Deed of Assignment and Waiver of Rights, surrendering Freedomland to the Philippine government for one peso.

Four years later, on June 11, 1978, Marcos issued Presidential Decree 1596 (PD 1596). PD 1596 did not lean on a romantic story about discovery. Its preamble justified Philippine sovereignty over the newly defined Kalayaan Island Group by citing security, economic interests, and proximity. The decree also drew coordinates that effectively excluded Itu Aba from Philippine claims—a significant concession to Taiwan. By doing that, it built a domestic legal object—Kalayaan Island Group—distinct from the broader "Spratlys" argument. It created a municipality of Kalayaan under Palawan.

In practice, our claim today is expressed through Pag-asa Island—the main inhabited island of Kalayaan. None of that leans on a romantic story about discovery. All of it leans on presence, administration, and legal acts. Kalayaan is ours to defend because we are actually there.

THE ONE THING MARCOLETA GOT RIGHT—AND WHAT HE'S DOING WITH IT

If you stack the documents, one narrow conclusion is hard to escape: Cloma did not "discover" an empty Kalayaan Island Group in 1956. The islands were not res nullius. On that specific historical and legal point, what Marcoleta said in the hearing—that treating Cloma's voyage as a "discovery" of an empty sea is inaccurate—is correct. But in other venues, Marcoleta has floated the idea of simply "giving up" the Kalayaan Island Group because the Cloma discovery myth doesn't hold up. That is a very different argument.

His line: "Ano ba talaga ang pagpapatayan natin? We will offer our lives to defend based on a fraudulent story?" Maritime law experts like Antonio Carpio and Jay Batongbacal have already dismantled that line, stressing that our claim rests on effective control—not Cloma's voyage.

He has also tried to reduce the work of people like Justice Carpio, Commodore Jay Tarriela, and Batongbacal to "satsat" (empty talk). Every time you call serious legal and strategic analysis "satsat," you are telling ordinary Filipinos to ignore the people defending our maritime rights. That weakens us from the inside.

Then there's the origin of some of his talking points. After a meeting with Chinese Ambassador Huang Xilian, Marcoleta went to the floor to relay Beijing's preferred framing on KIG. Pro-China outlets like Asian Century Journal, already flagged by security analysts for repeating Chinese narratives, have amplified his talking points.

So yes, on Cloma's "discovery," he stumbled into the right side of the historical record. But he's not using that truth to harden our legal footing on effective control. He's using it while pushing other lines that soften the public for giving up.

THE REAL RECEIPTS: WHY KALAYAAN IS LEGALLY OURS

If we want a real origin story, we don't need to start in 1956. We can look at the 1734 Murillo Velarde map, often called the "Mother of all Philippine Maps." Drawn in Manila by Father Pedro Murillo Velarde with Filipino artisans, it already shows the Spratly-area features as part of the Philippine archipelagic frame.

Then there's the 1900 Treaty of Washington, where Spain explicitly sold the Philippines to the United States, including islands that had been part of the Spanish colonial domain. In international law, there's also uti possidetis juris—when a colony becomes an independent state, the old colonial boundaries become the new state's international boundaries. If those colonial-era treaties and maps treated far-flung shoals and reefs as part of the Philippines, the successor state inherits that claim.

Fast forward to UNCLOS and the 2016 arbitral award. UNCLOS runs on a simple but powerful idea: land dominates the sea. Because we have a solid chain of colonial-era inclusion, post-colonial state succession, and decades of actual effective control—garrisoning, civilian settlement on Pag-asa, municipal governance—we have the receipts. When you stack up the 1734 map, the 1900 Treaty of Washington, colonial succession rules, continuous effective control through PD 1596 and military presence, and UNCLOS maritime rights, the claim stands without Cloma.

WHY WE NEED TO LET THE MYTH GO ANYWAY

We're in a strange place as a country. We keep defending a discovery myth the documents don't support, while our actual legal strength—effective control, state administration, colonial succession—sits underexplained. It makes us easier to embarrass in international debates, because rival states can point to French annexation notes, Vietnamese imperial decrees, and British guano leases and make Cloma look naive.

We can say: Cloma didn't discover Kalayaan; the sea was never empty; but we have been there for decades, we administer it, we have people living on Pag-asa, and we have colonial-era receipts that predate Cloma by centuries. Letting go of the myth is not surrendering to China or to any politician. It's tightening our own argument.

Kalayaan is ours to defend, just not for the reasons we were taught. And Marcoleta? He may have stumbled onto one argument that actually holds up. But I don't think he's using it to strengthen our position.

482:
[An MCT Commentary] Some of you asked why I reacted the way I did when I reposted the news about Toni Yulo-Loyzaga being brought back. Fair question. Let me answer it. But first, the usual reminder: this is just me, thinking out loud. This is my personal take, based on the data I track. I am not trying to tell you what to think. I genuinely don't recommend basing your judgments on what I write. What I do hope is that something here makes you curious enough to go look it up yourself. That's actually the whole point of MCT. If I can get you to pause and read more, I'm happy. Okay. Now to your question.

WHAT HAPPENED
Malacañang reappointed Maria Antonia "Toni" Yulo-Loyzaga as Special Envoy for Disaster Risk Reduction and Management. She was previously DENR Secretary under Marcos Jr. and left in 2025 when he reshuffled his Cabinet. Now she's back, this time with a different title but in a space that sits right at the center of climate negotiations, disaster funding, and international resilience deals. On paper, sure. She has a background in climate and disaster work. Manila Observatory, National Resilience Council, those are real credentials. I get why the Palace framed it the way they did. But I track people. And when I track her, here is what the data shows me.

BEFORE I GO FURTHER
I want to say this clearly: I am not saying she is guilty of any crime. I am not a court. I am not a prosecutor. When I look at her record, the pattern I see raises serious questions about whether this appointment makes sense for a role that demands "unimpeachable" public trust. That pattern is what I want to walk you through.

PALAWAN: THE RANCH, THE RESERVE, AND THE QUESTION NOBODY ANSWERED
In 1975, Proclamation 1387 created the Busuanga Pasture Reserve in Busuanga and Coron, Palawan. Around 40,000 hectares of state-owned grazing land, roughly ten times the size of Manila. Public land. Government property. Around 1976, the Yulo family, through Yulo King Ranch or YKR, secured control over that same area through government pasture arrangements. After 1986, that tract became the subject of sequestration proceedings, ill-gotten wealth questions, and was tagged in some quarters as the largest agrarian anomaly in the country. Fast forward to today: the Department of Agrarian Reform says parts of the YKR area are covered by CARP petitions and agrarian reform distribution processes. Their position is that this is public pasture land under a government agreement, not a clean, uncontested private estate. A farmers' group called Katipunan ng mga Magbubukid sa Busuanga filed land-grabbing and ethics complaints naming Yulo-Loyzaga in connection with the ranch. One case was brought to the Ombudsman in 2023 alleging conflict of interest and ethical violations. In Congress, the Makabayan bloc and Representative France Castro filed resolutions calling for a probe into the conflict-of-interest risk of having a DENR Secretary whose family is historically tied to a disputed reserve that DENR itself oversees. Senator Raffy Tulfo filed a similar resolution. Now here is her side, and I want to be fair about this: she has denied all of it, clearly and repeatedly. She says the Busuanga Pasture Reserve has always been government-owned, that her family never owned it, and that the complaints are politically motivated. The questions were filed, the complaints were raised, Congress wanted a probe, and none of it was ever fully resolved. Conflict of interest goes beyond titles and ownership. Trust is the real issue. Can the public reasonably trust that her decisions were made without fear or favor? That question was never answered. And now she has a new post.

CHOCOLATE HILLS: THE PAPERWORK WAS THERE, THE PROTECTION WASN'T
The Chocolate Hills in Bohol are a National Geological Monument, protected under Proclamation 1037. Not a gray area. A clearly protected site. Captain's Peak Garden and Resort built inside the vicinity of the Chocolate Hills. Some of the hills were physically cut to fit the resort's design. The whole thing went viral in March 2024 and people were furious. Here is where I want to be precise, because the full picture is more complicated than expected. The records show that DENR actually issued a Temporary Closure Order against the resort on 6 September. They followed that with a Notice of Violation on 22 January 2024 for operating without an Environmental compliance. So the paperwork existed. But the resort was still operating, or had resumed operating, by the time the videos hit social media. That is the problem. Yulo-Loyzaga herself went on site and admitted that some hills were damaged. Officials tried to explain how local permits were issued by pointing to land classification and private property arguments. The orders existed. But the orders did not stop the damage. For a DENR Secretary, the job is not just to file paperwork. It is to make sure the protection actually works. That did not happen here.

MASUNGI: WHO DOES THE LAW PROTECT?
Masungi Georeserve in Baras, Rizal is one of the better-known conservation success stories in recent years. It was built on a 2017 MOA that former DENR Secretary Gina Lopez signed, covering around 2,700 hectares. Under Yulo-Loyzaga, DENR formally terminated the older 2002 joint venture contract that underlies the Masungi arrangement. In Senate hearings, she held the line: Masungi is not a special case, everyone has to comply with the law. From a purely legal standpoint, that argument is not crazy. Irregular contracts are irregular contracts, even when the project they support is doing good work. But here is what Masungi's supporters say, and this includes the Masungi Georeserve Foundation and many conservation groups: that while DENR was pressing hard on Masungi's legal technicalities, quarrying interests and other actors were not held to the same standard. I'll be honest: I don't know who is completely right here. This is a genuine legal and policy dispute. The optics, though, sent a clear message to conservation groups. There is a big difference between enforcing the law consistently across the board and enforcing it selectively.

MINING AND THE DEVELOPMENT TILT
When she was first appointed in 2022, the framing from Malacañang was about harnessing mineral resources. She publicly supported small-scale mining with LGU-level monitoring. A wide range of environmental groups, NGOs, and people's organizations have consistently criticized the direction her DENR took. Groups have called on her successor to revoke reclamation projects, seabed quarrying permits, and other approvals made under her watch. She was not pro-mining at all costs. But a broad cross-section of civil society experienced her DENR as development-tilted.

TRAVEL, PERFORMANCE, AND A RETURN WITHOUT CLOSURE
Her foreign travel and participation in international climate and disaster forums became a sustained criticism. It did not always translate well even in those international spaces. At COP28, NGOs specifically complained that the Philippine pavilion under her leadership sidelined vulnerable communities and centered corporate and government voices. That is not a small critique. COP is precisely where countries are supposed to show up for the people most at risk from climate change. By 2025, Marcos replaced her with Raphael Lotilla in a Cabinet reset. Political reporting framed her exit as quiet, professional. She left with Palawan unresolved, Chocolate Hills still a fresh wound, Masungi still contested, and COP28's NGO critique unanswered. And now she is back.

SO WHY DID I REACT THAT WAY
Disaster Risk Reduction is not a ceremonial post. It sits at the intersection of climate finance, land policy, and international credibility. A Special Envoy in this space talks to donors, lenders, and international bodies. They shape where the money goes and what the Philippines commits to. When I track a public official, I look for patterns. Here is what I found: - Ongoing allegations and investigations about conflict of interest around the YKR–Busuanga Pasture... - A DENR tenure where the paperwork existed but the protection sometimes didn't, where Gina Lopez's conservation legacy was partially reversed... - A COP28 appearance where NGOs said vulnerable communities were displaced in favor of corporate space... That is the pattern. That is why I reacted. But let me be crystal about what this pattern really points to. As I mentioned in my post yesterday, this country is not running out of talent. There are so many excellent names in the scientific and resilience spaces, experts who do the quiet, unglamorous work. Choosing to recycle a controversial figure instead of tapping fresh, unburdened talent is a deliberate political choice. My criticism of this appointment is similar to my criticism of the reappointment of Christina Frasco. It is less about the appointee, and more about the machinery doing the appointing. When the administration makes a move like this, it tells us that political accommodation is still being prioritized over performance accountability. My worry? This country loses lives every single year to typhoons, floods, and landslides. The people who represent us in disaster and climate spaces should walk in with their records clean, their conflicts resolved, and their credibility intact. If that is the standard, this appointment does not meet it. That is my take, and it is only my take, based on publicly reported facts, official statements, and my own reading of the patterns. Go check the facts yourself. Tell me where I'm wrong. I mean that.

SIX MONTHS, NINE REFERRALS, ZERO BIG FISH IN DETENTION — THE ICI CLOSES SHOP

The Independent Commission for Infrastructure operated for exactly six months. In that time, two of its three commissioners quit, it lost the ability to take official actions for the last three of those months, and the vast majority of the politicians it implicated remain free.

On March 13, 2026, ICI Chairperson Andres Reyes Jr. — a retired Associate Justice of the Supreme Court and the only remaining member of the body — issued a statement recommending the commission wind down operations. It will remain open through March 31 to wrap up paperwork, then go dark.

President Marcos had already signaled this outcome two days earlier, telling reporters in New York that the ICI's "brief has already been fulfilled."

WHAT THE ICI WAS AND WHAT IT WAS SUPPOSED TO DO

Executive Order No. 94, signed September 11, 2025, created the ICI as an ad hoc fact-finding body to investigate anomalies in government flood control and infrastructure projects going back 10 years.

The body had no prosecutorial power. It could gather evidence, issue subpoenas, recommend charges, and refer cases to the Office of the Ombudsman and the Department of Justice.

It was created after public outrage over Marcos's July 2025 State of the Nation Address, in which he declared that 5,500 flood control projects had been completed. Typhoon Carina flooding days later made those claims difficult to defend. Finance Secretary Ralph Recto told the Senate that up to 118.5 billion pesos in flood control funds may have been lost to corruption over just two years.

WHAT IT ACTUALLY ACCOMPLISHED

The ICI submitted nine referrals to the Ombudsman covering 65 individuals, and coordinated referrals of 66 more to the Department of Justice. The Anti-Money Laundering Council froze around 25 billion pesos in assets. The commission convened 20 government agencies into a Technical Working Group for Asset Recovery and developed three information systems for detecting fraud in infrastructure projects.

On March 12, two police vehicles transported ICI documents and evidence to the Ombudsman's office in Quezon City.

Among those implicated: former Senator Bong Revilla (plunder charges recommended), Senators Joel Villanueva and Jinggoy Estrada, former House Speaker Martin Romualdez (referred for further investigation), former DPWH Secretary Manuel Bonoan, and contractor Zaldy Co, declared a fugitive from justice by the Sandiganbayan in December 2025. Senators Francis Escudero, Nancy Binay, Grace Poe, and Mark Villar were referred for further investigation.

Of that list, only a handful of contractors and former district engineers have actually been detained.

THE BODY THAT COLLAPSED FROM WITHIN

The ICI started with three commissioners. By January 2026, it was down to one.

Former DPWH Secretary Rogelio Singson — public works chief from 2010 to 2016 — resigned effective December 15, 2025, citing stress and security concerns. Before leaving, he sent a text message to Caloocan Rep. Edgar Erice asking why he should "risk life and family's safety to resolve Malacañang's problems." He warned the commission needed stronger statutory subpoena powers and stronger backing from the executive and Congress.

Rossana Fajardo, a partner at SGV and Co. (EY Philippines) and the commission's forensic finance expert, submitted her resignation on December 26, effective December 31. She said the investigative work should now transition to the DOJ and Ombudsman.

With two departures and no replacement appointments from the Palace, the ICI formally acknowledged in January 2026 that it could not conduct official actions. Under EO 94, it needed a majority — two of three commissioners — to take any binding step. Reyes was alone.

Erice, a Liberal Party representative, told a House committee in February that he would characterize the ICI as "a total failure, especially considering the resignations of two of its members." The Makabayan bloc warned after Singson's exit that the investigation would only reach "minor offenders while shielding those in power."

In February, Reyes said the body "could have done more if it were intact, and will do its task of investigating and referring cases related to the flood control scandal and fighting corruption to the very end, if allowed." That phrase — "if allowed" — went unaddressed.

WHAT HAPPENS NEXT

Ombudsman Jesus Crispin Remulla — the former Justice Secretary whom Marcos appointed to the post in October 2025 — now holds the ICI's documents and referrals. The DOJ receives the same materials. The Technical Working Group for Asset Recovery continues its work, having been explicitly called on by Reyes to keep going after the commission closes.

Two replacement bodies are pending in Congress: the Independent Commission Against Infrastructure Corruption, and the Independent People's Commission. Reyes called on Congress to pass both bills. Neither has been enacted.

Reyes submitted a 1,000-page accomplishment report to Malacañang on February 6, covering the ICI's first 125 days. Concerns were raised by the Daily Tribune that this report may have been softened before any public release, though no official confirmation of that has been made.

The flood control scandal that triggered the ICI involves projects that consumed hundreds of billions of pesos across multiple administrations. The ICI produced nine referrals in six months and handed everything to the Ombudsman.

Whether Remulla's office, the DOJ, and the courts will carry that work forward — or whether the referrals become another stack of documents in another government building — is the question that the wind-down leaves open.

THE SUPREME COURT JUST LOCKED IN THE DOJ'S HIGHER EVIDENCE STANDARD FOR CRIMINAL CASES

93.9 percent. That is the conviction rate the Department of Justice is now posting after raising the bar for what it takes to file a criminal case in the Philippines. Before the change, the figure hovered between 38 and 39 percent — meaning more than six out of ten criminal cases that reached court ended without a conviction.

The Supreme Court, in a unanimous En Banc ruling promulgated November 11, 2025 and released to the public on March 11, 2026, dismissed a petition challenging that higher standard and upheld the DOJ's authority to set it. The decision in G.R. No. 280455 — Atty. Hazel L. Meking v. Secretary Jesus Crispin C. Remulla — is six pages long and written by Associate Justice Japar B. Dimaampao. It is not a close call.

WHAT DOJ CIRCULAR 15 ACTUALLY CHANGED

Before July 2024, prosecutors deciding whether to file criminal charges operated under probable cause — essentially, a reasonable belief that a crime occurred and that the person charged was likely responsible. The bar was deliberately low. Courts, not prosecutors, were supposed to sort out admissibility and credibility of testimony. Getting a case to court was the point. Winning it later was a separate question.

Department Circular No. 015, Series of 2024 — signed by then-DOJ Secretary Jesus Crispin Remulla on July 16, 2024, with President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. present at the signing — replaced that standard with "prima facie evidence with reasonable certainty of conviction." Under the new rules, a prosecutor must be convinced that all available evidence is admissible, credible, and capable of being preserved and introduced in court. If it falls short, the complaint can be dismissed before it is even formally docketed. A case build-up stage now precedes docketing, where the head of the prosecution office assesses whether the evidence is complete enough to proceed.

The result, according to then-Justice Undersecretary Jesse Hermogenes Andres in a November 2025 Senate hearing, is that the DOJ is filing 50 percent fewer cases. But the cases that do get filed are winning at a 93.9 percent rate.

THE PETITION AND THE COURT'S ANSWER

Atty. Hazel L. Meking filed the petition on June 4, 2025, on behalf of crime victims whose complaints had already been dismissed under the new standard. Her core legal argument: by raising the evidentiary bar in preliminary investigations, the DOJ encroached on the Supreme Court's exclusive constitutional authority under Article VIII, Section 5(5) of the Constitution to promulgate rules on pleading, practice, and procedure in courts.

Her practical argument was blunter. She argued it is impossible to assess certainty of conviction before any witness is examined, before evidence is formally tested, before a trial is conducted. She also documented inconsistent implementation: some prosecution offices dismiss cases on first look without any hearing; others close cases through internal committees with no hearing at all. A crime victim in one province may get a different outcome than an identical victim in a neighboring city.

The Supreme Court was not persuaded. The decision draws a clear line between executive and judicial functions. Preliminary investigation — the stage before charges are formally filed in court — is an executive process. The SC retains supreme authority over what happens in courts and the power to intervene if prosecutorial rules impair constitutional rights. But the DOJ, operating within its own domain, can set its own rules for the pre-court stage.

The ruling also pointed to precedent that made the constitutional question almost moot: in 2024, before Meking's petition was even filed, the SC had already issued an administrative resolution (A.M. No. 24-02-09-SC) recognizing the DOJ's authority and ordering that portions of Rule 112 of the Rules of Criminal Procedure inconsistent with the new DOJ rules be deemed repealed. The court had already weighed in — in the DOJ's favor — before the case was brought.

WHAT THIS MEANS IN PRACTICE

The Supreme Court decision does not resolve the tension Meking identified. It resolves the constitutional question. Whether the standard is being applied consistently and fairly across every prosecution office in the country is a separate problem, and the ruling does not touch it.

The circular covers any crime with a prescribed penalty of at least six years and one day. That includes plunder, graft and corruption, murder, kidnapping, rape, major drug offenses, and violations of the Anti-Money Laundering Act. For anyone filing a complaint in these categories, the evidence burden now begins at the prosecutor's desk, not in the courtroom. A complaint that would have cleared the old probable cause threshold may now be sent back before it is even docketed.

The DOJ argues this is the point. Marcos said at the signing ceremony that "only strong cases are brought to trial," reducing frivolous suits and malicious prosecutions. Remulla — now Ombudsman of the Philippines — called the circular a "game-changer in the prosecution of crimes." The DOJ's institutional target is a 90 percent conviction rate, and the numbers suggest it is close.

For crime victims who can gather strong, admissible, documented evidence, the system now leads more reliably to a conviction. For victims who cannot — those who depend on testimony alone, or who lack the resources to build a complete evidentiary record before filing — the pre-docketing stage is now a gate that did not exist before.

The Supreme Court has confirmed that gate is lawful. Whether it is applied uniformly is another question entirely.

NEARLY A TON OF SHABU, A GROUNDED BOAT, AND A SYNDICATE THAT SENT DRONES TO FIND IT

The speedboat hit rocks somewhere along the shoreline of Sitio Agbalite, Barangay Harrison, Paluan, Occidental Mindoro at around 3 in the afternoon on March 10. Its hull punctured, the vessel ran aground. Two men on board — later identified as Chinese nationals — refused help from local residents who approached the stranded craft. They could not communicate. They did not speak Tagalog. When soldiers from the nearby 76th Infantry Battalion patrol base arrived minutes after residents reported the scene, the two men fled toward the mountains.

The soldiers inspected the boat anyway. Inside, they found 43 sacks.

WHAT WAS IN THOSE SACKS

The sacks contained methamphetamine hydrochloride — shabu — with a confirmed purity of 90 percent, according to PDEA Mimaropa lab results. The sacks bore Vietnamese-language markings. PDEA Director General Isagani Nerez said the drugs did not originate from the Philippines.

The initial army field estimate put the weight at 2.2 metric tons — a calculation based on 50 kilograms per sack. That figure was wrong. When PDEA physically weighed the contents, each sack came in at roughly 20 to 25 kilograms. The actual confirmed weight, as announced by DILG Secretary Jonvic Remulla at a press conference in Calapan City on March 13, is 986 kilograms. The street value, per official figures, falls between P5.8 billion and P6.8 billion.

The speedboat also carried a Starlink satellite communication device, hand flares, fire extinguishers, batteries, and life-saving equipment. The boat is registered to CrystalWave Services LTD. Inc., with a business address in Olongapo City, Zambales.

THE ARRESTS

The two Chinese nationals spent the night in the mountains. On March 11, at around 5 PM, troops from the 5th Infantry Battalion, working with Military Intelligence and PNP Special Action Force personnel, captured them in Barangay Alipa, Paluan. Major General Ramon Zagala, commander of the 2nd Infantry Division, confirmed the arrests: "This afternoon, we captured the two Chinese individuals who escaped yesterday."

They were identified as Xing Xing Zhan, 38, married and unemployed, from Anhui, China, and Bin Shi, 36, separated and working as a freelancer, from Hubei, China. Both arrived in the Philippines on March 5 — five days before the operation. Neither cooperated with investigators. An interpreter was brought in.

A day later, on March 12 at 7:17 AM, a Philippine Navy patrol vessel, BRP Pandanan, intercepted a twin-engine rubber boat approaching Paluan from the sea. Aboard were two Filipino boatmen from Nasugbu, Batangas. They told authorities they had been hired by someone they knew as "Wendy Que," who they believed owned the grounded speedboat, to find and rescue the two Chinese nationals. Both were taken into custody.

Four arrests total. One female Chinese national believed to be the group's leader remains at large.

DRONES, A MOTHERSHIP, AND FOUR MONTHS OF PREPARATION

Remulla said the syndicate deployed drones over Paluan to track the drugs after the boat ran aground and the suspects fled. "When their vessel ran aground and they were pursued, they utilized drones to locate the remaining drugs," he said. "These are not typical criminals. They are part of syndicates."

The suspected method: a larger vessel transfers the drugs in international waters to a smaller speedboat, which then brings the load ashore. The drugs move from a mothership to the coast with no direct trace back to the source.

The unnamed female leader had been renting a house in Batangas since November 2025 — the operation was months in the making before the March 10 attempt.

MINDORO AS A RECURRING ENTRY POINT

This is not the first time drug syndicates have tried to use Mindoro. In February 2025, the Philippine Coast Guard and PDEA intercepted a vessel near Mindoro's exclusive economic zone carrying an estimated 5 tons of shabu valued at P34 billion. That vessel turned back when it spotted a PCG ship. The drugs were never physically seized.

"Syndicates always plan on landing in Mindoro," Nerez said at the March 13 press conference.

The March 2026 Paluan seizure is one of the largest single-vessel drug hauls in Philippine history, though it does not exceed the P11 billion worth of shabu intercepted in Infanta, Quezon in March 2022 — still the record for the largest physical drug seizure in the country.

WHAT THE GOVERNMENT IS SAYING

PNP Chief General Jose Melencio Nartatez Jr. was at the Calapan City press conference, where sacks of shabu were displayed to media. Remulla named the culprit directly: a Chinese-led international drug syndicate. "We are not angry at the Chinese people or at China," he said. "We are angry at the criminal Chinese who come here."

PDEA announced plans to coordinate with foreign counterparts to trace the drug origins and the syndicate behind the shipment. Remulla said the government will strengthen maritime monitoring through long-range drones and satellite technology.

THE THREAD THAT IS STILL OPEN

The investigation is ongoing as of March 13. Wendy Que, described as the speedboat owner who directed the Filipino boatmen, has been identified but not publicly named. The Bureau of Immigration is still verifying the status of the arrested Chinese nationals.

The female Chinese national who allegedly led the group — the one who spent four months in Batangas, the one the drones were presumably trying to help — has not been found.

PART 3:   CHECK YOUR BIAS BEFORE YOU HIT "POST"

I caught myself doing it last week.

A friend shared a post about a politician I already disliked. I didn't read the article. I didn't check the source. I saw the headline, felt a rush of validation, and my thumb was already hovering over the share button.

Then I stopped. Because the headline was from a page I'd never heard of, and the "source" was a screenshot of a tweet that no longer existed. I almost spread something I couldn't verify, all because it confirmed what I already believed.

Medyo napahiya ako sa sarili ko.

And that's the thing. We think we're being smart when we react fast. We think speed means conviction. But most of the time, speed just means we didn't think.

This piece is for every Filipino who has ever posted, shared, commented, or reacted to anything political online. Which is basically all of us. The goal here isn't to make you feel guilty. It's to make you harder to fool.

I also need to be clear about where I stand. MCT is critical of the Dutertes, especially Sara. That didn’t come from one viral clip or one chismis thread. It came from years of watching her behavior, studying her statements, tracking her decisions, and looking at how her family has used power.

I unfollowed almost all political analysts so that when I write, I’m not borrowing anybody’s script. My bias is real, but it’s rooted in patterns and data, not in loyalty to another politician.

There were moments when I wanted to believe a juicy anti‑Duterte story, only to find out Vera Files or Rappler had already debunked it. Those are the moments that remind me: even when I’m critical of them, I still owe them accuracy.

YOUR BRAIN IS WIRED TO TAKE SHORTCUTS

Before we even get to politics, let's talk about what's happening inside your head.

Daniel Kahneman, a psychologist who won the Nobel Prize in Economics, described two systems in our brain.

System 1 is fast, automatic, and emotional.
System 2 is slow, deliberate, and logical. Most of the time,

System 1 runs the show. It makes snap judgments. It fills in the blanks. It decides if something "feels true" before System 2 even wakes up.

This is useful when you're crossing the street or catching a ball. It's dangerous when you're reading a political post at 11 PM and your blood is already boiling.

Psychologists call this an "amygdala hijack." Your brain's emotional alarm system fires before your rational brain can process what's actually happening.

Adrenaline floods in. Rational thinking from the prefrontal cortex temporarily shuts down.

And in that moment, you're not thinking. You're reacting.

The good news: it only takes about six seconds for those chemicals to dissipate.

Six seconds.

That's the gap between a thoughtful response and a comment you'll regret at 3 AM.

THE FOUR BIASES THAT CONTROL YOUR FEED

Here's where it gets specific. These aren't abstract psychology terms. These are patterns you'll recognize in yourself if you're honest enough to look.

⬜ Confirmation bias. This is the big one. It means you naturally look for, believe, and remember information that supports what you already think. If you're anti-Marcos, you'll scroll past ten neutral stories and stop at the one that confirms your suspicion.

If you're pro-Duterte, you'll share the post that defends him without checking if the data is real.

A study on Filipino student voters at Laguna State Polytechnic University found a significant relationship between social media behavior, confirmation bias, and political awareness.

The more active the students were online, the more their biases shaped what they believed to be true.

⬜ The halo effect. This one is sneaky. If you like a politician's personality, you'll assume they're also smart, honest, and competent.

Research shows that even physical attractiveness affects how we judge a leader's knowledge and ability.

If someone is charming on camera, we forgive a lot. If someone is awkward, we assume the worst.

Think about how differently Filipinos react to the exact same policy depending on who announces it.

⬜ The bandwagon effect. "Lahat sila galit, so tama sigurong magalit din ako."

This is the pressure to adopt a belief just because it seems like everyone around you already holds it.

Psychologists describe it as a cognitive shortcut: instead of evaluating information yourself, you let the crowd decide for you.

It's why hashtags trend before anyone verifies the story behind them. It's why a post with 50,000 angry reactions feels more "true" than a fact-check with 200 shares.

⬜ Emotional reasoning. Aaron Beck, the founder of cognitive behavioral therapy, called this "mistaking feelings for facts."

You feel angry, so the post must be offensive. You feel scared, so the claim must be real. You feel attacked, so the person must be wrong. Research shows emotional reasoning is linked to anxiety, depression, and distorted perception of reality.

Online, it turns every disagreement into a war.

HOW PHILIPPINE POLITICS MAKES IT WORSE

Our political culture is a breeding ground for unchecked bias.

Start with pakikisama. We defend people in our group even when they're wrong, because loyalty is currency. If someone in your circle supports a candidate, disagreeing feels like betrayal. So you stay quiet. Or worse, you agree publicly and doubt privately.

Then there's utang na loob. If a politician did something for your family, your barangay, your province, that debt follows you into the comment section. You'll defend them not because of evidence, but because of obligation.

And hiya. The fear of being called out. Of being labeled "dilawan" or "DDS" or "troll" depending on which side you accidentally offend. So you self-censor, or you double down on your position to avoid looking weak.

Now layer all of that onto a social media environment where troll farms are an actual industry.

The BBC documented how political operatives in the Philippines hire people to manage hundreds of fake Facebook profiles and spread disinformation for candidates.

A Laguna State Polytechnic study confirmed that cognitive biases and social media behavior are directly linked to how politically aware (or misled) Filipino voters become.

Lawmakers have even filed House Bill 11178 to criminalize troll farm operations because the problem has gotten that severe.

That’s why fact-checkers have become targets themselves.

Vera Files, Rappler, and the wider Tsek.ph coalition have all faced harassment, legal threats, and smears from allies of those they repeatedly debunk, especially under and after Duterte.

You're not just fighting your own bias. You're fighting a system designed to exploit it.

WHAT’S ON YOU, WHAT’S NOT

It’s important to be fair here.

You are responsible for what you like, share, and post. Walang ibang may hawak ng account mo. You decide whether you amplify something or let it die in your notifications.

But you’re not the one building the algorithms, funding the troll farms, or scripting the propaganda videos. Platforms and political operators poured money and tech into making sure your worst mental shortcuts get triggered as often as possible. They benefit from you being tired, emotional, and distracted.

Your small choices won’t fix the whole system. They can, however, keep you from becoming raw material for it.

THE 10-SECOND TEST BEFORE YOU REACT

Here's something practical. Before you post, share, comment, or react to anything political, run through these five questions. It takes ten seconds.

Am I reacting to the content, or to who posted it? If your enemy shared a fact, it's still a fact. If your idol shared a lie, it's still a lie.

If the roles were reversed, would I react the same way? This is the hardest one. If the politician you like did the exact same thing as the politician you hate, would you still be outraged? Be honest.

What emotion am I feeling right now? Name it. Galit. Takot. Hiya. Pride. Psychologists say the simple act of naming an emotion activates your rational brain and weakens the amygdala's grip. It sounds small. It works.

Did I read the full article, or just the headline and comments? A study from Indiana University found that political bias on social media comes primarily from user behavior, not from the platforms themselves. We build our own echo chambers by who we follow and what we click. The algorithm just amplifies what we already chose.

Have I seen this from at least one other independent source? If you can only find the claim on one page, one blog, or one screenshot, pause.

At minimum, check one fact-checking outlet. In the Philippines, that can mean Tsek.ph’s coalition site, Vera Files, Rappler’s fact check desk, or any of the other partners that have been tracking political lies for years.

Tsek.ph is a joint fact-checking project of universities, newsrooms, and civil society groups that verifies candidates’ claims, campaign promises, and viral posts, especially during elections.

Vera Files is an independent, non-profit newsroom and a Facebook third‑party fact-checking partner, with years of data showing that many of Duterte’s checked statements were false.

Rappler built a digital forensics team and helped launch #FactsFirstPH, a network that published hundreds of fact-checks in 2022 and continues to track coordinated disinformation.

Tsek.ph’s election reports show disinformation often comes from or benefits powerful campaigns, not just random meme pages.

Vera Files’ yearenders found a large share of its fact checks on public officials focused on Duterte’s inaccurate statements. Running a viral claim through sources like these before reacting isn’t paranoia; it’s the bare minimum in a country where disinformation is a political tool.

WHAT HONEST OPINION ACTUALLY LOOKS LIKE

There's a difference between having a position and being manipulated into one.

I’m saying this as someone who already has a strong position. I am anti-Duterte and anti-Marcos. I don’t hide that.

I’ve spent too many hours reading documents, watching interviews, and connecting timelines to pretend I’m neutral about what their family has done with power.

But even with that history, I still have to check myself.

When a new story about them appears, I have to ask: am I seeing this clearly, or am I too eager to believe the worst because it fits the picture I already have?

That’s one reason I stopped following most political analysts. I want my take to come from the source material and the pattern of behavior, not from someone else’s outrage or branding.

If a new document or investigation came out tomorrow that challenged my view of the Dutertes, my job wouldn’t be to spin it away. My job would be to sit with it, even if it complicates the story I’ve already told.

An honest opinion sounds like: "I oppose this policy because the data shows X, and here's what I've read." A biased reaction sounds like: "Bobo admin, period."

An honest opinion leaves room for: "I might be wrong about this part." A biased reaction can't tolerate doubt.

The goal isn't to be neutral. Neutrality in the face of corruption or injustice is its own kind of failure. The goal is to know why you believe what you believe, and to make sure that reason isn't just "because it felt true" or "because everyone on my feed said so."

MediaSmarts, a digital literacy organization, puts it simply: people who have received formal critical thinking education are 26 percent less likely to believe in conspiracy theories.

People who merely identify as "critical thinkers" without that training are 63 percent more likely to believe them.

Calling yourself maalam doesn't make you maalam. The work is in the checking, not the declaring.

SMALL HABITS THAT ACTUALLY HELP

Follow at least one source you disagree with. Not to hate-read. To understand how the other side builds its arguments. You don't have to agree. You have to listen.

When you feel triggered, type your reaction in Notes first. Not in the comment section. Breathe. Wait six seconds. Then decide if it's worth posting.

Practice the reverse test once a week. Find a political story you agree with, and ask yourself: would I accept this if it came from the other side? If the answer is no, you've found a bias worth examining.

Talk to one real person offline about a political issue instead of fighting with strangers online. It's harder to dehumanize someone sitting across from you.

And when you're wrong, say it. Publicly. It doesn't make you weak. It makes you the kind of person who's harder to manipulate next time.

Kasi ang totoo, walang perfectly unbiased na tao. Lahat tayo may kinakampihan. The question is whether you're aware of it, or whether someone else is using it against you without you knowing.

If you want a place to start, don’t change your whole behavior yet. Just do one thing today: before you share anything political, ask yourself that one question — “Have I checked this even once?” If the answer is no, stop there. That tiny pause already makes you harder to use.

SOURCES:

https://docs.google.com/document/d/1hyfRWpxqic6RC68ysHe9pfE_WkbisdWPA_T3C26nguw/edit?usp=sharing

You’re looking at the comment section and something feels off.

The names look normal. The profile photos look like real people. The tone is polite. No one is shouting. No one is threatening anyone.

But when you read them in one go, you will notice the script.

Romy: “Accusations and allegations are easy. Proving them true is a different story.”
Angela: “di ako naniniwala na corrupt si mark villar..”
Delfin: “KUNG CORRUPT TALAGA SI VILLAR MATAGAL NA SANA SYA NAKASUHAN!!”
Felipe: “Accusations pa lang wla pa conviction. Let his track record speak for itself!”
Jessel: “Hangga’t walang paper trail and bank records im not convinced”
Camille: “MAS MADALI KASI MAGBINTANG KESA MAGPATUNAY!!”
Gina: “Puro akusasyon and paninira lang kasi malapit sila kay duterte…”
Mary: “Walang documents or paper trail, walang pera, walang kaso. Tapos!”

Different mouths. Same sentence. Same idea, written in a slightly different way.

The message is always the same: accusations are easy, there is no paper trail or case, so it’s unfair to even suspect corruption.

They don’t talk about documents, or timelines, or behavior. Ang china‑challenge nila is not the evidence, but your right to feel bothered in the first place.

That is the soft troll.

The screenshot you’re seeing here is from an old post I wrote about Mark Villar.

It wasn’t a one‑time thing. I’ve seen the same script appear under other posts too, and most of those accounts are already blocked on this page.

These pests aren’t welcome here. I’m sharing this now so you can see how they move, and so the next time they show up on your feed, you know what you’re looking at.

The Loud Troll Never Left

On any given day, if you poke at politics online, you still bump into the classic troll.

All caps. “BOBO.” “NPA.” “Bayaran.” Threats. Personal insults. If you’re a woman, add misogyny on top.

Years of reporting on Philippine “cyber troops” describe the same thing: paid or coordinated accounts harassing critics, journalists, and activists as part of organized political campaigns.

Analysts now call this “digital proxy warfare” here — online haters acting as foot soldiers for politicians and power blocs, waging war in the comments and quote tweets so the politicians who hired them don’t have to get their hands dirty.

The targets haven’t changed much: opposition figures, human rights workers, community organizers, good governance bloggers and vloggers, even ordinary citizens who happen to go viral with the “wrong” opinion.

That loud troll exists to scare you, exhaust you, and drown you out.

But the industry didn’t stop there.

The Soft Troll Is The Upgrade (?)

Alongside the loud one, a quieter version evolved.

Calm tone. Semi‑formal English with Tagalog sprinkled in. A reasonable‑sounding appeal to fairness.

“Let’s wait for evidence.”
“Walang kaso, so don’t judge.”
“He/she did a lot of good things, it’s unfair to focus on the negative.”

Globally, disinformation researchers have a category for this: coordinated inauthentic behavior and comment seeding — batches of accounts dropping similar, organic‑looking remarks to steer the tone of a discussion.

In Southeast Asia and especially in the Philippines, these are part of what scholars describe as “cyber troops”: structured teams using fake accounts, paid influencers, and now AI tools to manipulate public opinion.

The idea is simple. Instead of screaming at you, they crowd you.

You open your own page, post something uncomfortable for a politician, and within a short window you get a wave of gentle, almost pastoral reminders to be fair.

How You Know It’s Organized

We all have friends who will genuinely defend their candidate. Hindi lahat ng pro comment, troll. The point is to learn the difference between organic disagreement and an operation.

Look at the profiles: thin but polished. Names that sound local. Faces that look real but might be AI‑generated or stolen. Locked timelines. Less than 100 friends. Less than 100 posts. Manila, Cebu, Davao, “Philippines” in the bio. Enough normal content — family, food, Bible verses — to pass a quick glance, but most of the visible activity is non political.

Look at the timing: comments landing within seconds of each other, all pushing the same angle.

This is the same pattern platforms cite when they take down Philippine networks for “coordinated inauthentic behavior”: multiple accounts posting the same themes in the same short windows to artificially amplify a message.

Work on our recent elections describes the same bursts timed with hearings, exposés, or campaign events.

Look at the kind of argument: it sounds logical but never touches the messy details. “Accusations pa lang, wala pang conviction.” “Hangga’t walang bank records, I’m not convinced.”

In a country where cases against powerful people almost never prosper, “walang kaso” is not neutral information; it’s part of the system.

Disinformation studies call this a plausibility frame — using technically true but incomplete statements to shut down uncomfortable questions.

When you put those together — thin locked profiles, synchronized timing, and copy‑paste logic — you’re not looking at eight random commenters anymore. You’re looking at an organized response.

Why Doubt Is The Product

The loud troll tried to make you believe a lie.

The soft troll is more patient. It’s happy if you end up believing nothing.

Researchers who track information operations worldwide say the goal is often to flood the zone with doubt.

If every accusation becomes “just politics,” if every document is “spin,” if every critic is “bitter” or “bayaran,” then nobody has to answer anything.

The soft troll leans hard on our own values to get there.

Filipinos don’t like being unfair. We’re raised with pakikisama, hiya, delicadeza. You don’t accuse someone lightly. You don’t jump to conclusions. You give people the benefit of the doubt.

These are good instincts. They keep communities from turning into lynch mobs.

But in the hands of a troll farm, those instincts become armor for the powerful.

So the script goes: you’re not corrupt, you’re being persecuted; critics aren’t doing accountability, they’re doing paninira; the real problem isn’t the alleged abuse of power, the real problem is people “judging too easily.”

The win is not to clear anyone’s name in a court of law.

The win is to make ordinary people shrug and say, “Ewan, ang gulo. Wala naman sigurong makukulong. Move on na lang.”

This Isn’t Just Paranoia

If this still sounds exaggerated, look at how institutions are reacting.

International outlets and researchers now treat Philippine political trolling as a case study.

The Diplomat calls it the globalization of Philippine political trolling, describing professionalized networks of paid workers, influencers, and fake accounts whose job is to steer online conversations for clients.

N3Con’s report on fact‑checkers in the country bluntly says troll farms are miles ahead of efforts to catch up.

Our own government acknowledges the problem when it wants to.

The Department of Information and Communications Technology launched Digital Bayanihan with social media platforms in 2025 to fight trolls, fake news, and deepfakes ahead of the elections — a formal admission that organized online manipulation is a national concern, not just chismis.

Security‑focused think tanks describe what’s happening here as the same digital proxy warfare mentioned earlier, with political and even foreign actors waging influence campaigns through online surrogates.

Academic work on authoritarian digital actors lists the Philippines as a place where cyber troops and coordinated campaigns are deeply embedded in everyday online life.

In other words: your gut feeling when you see that Villar thread on my page is backed by research. You’re not imagining it. You’re just seeing what they were paid to hide.

What You Can Do, Without Burning Out

You don’t have to turn into a one‑person fact‑checking org. Hindi mo kailangan sagutin bawat isa.

Start with awareness.

When you see a wave of comments that defend a politician using the same fairness language, coming from thin locked profiles, posted within the same hour — name it for what it is. You’re likely looking at soft trolls, part of the same coordinated inauthentic behavior and cyber troop activity described in studies on our own country.

You can call the pattern, not the individual. “Napapansin niyo ba, sabay‑sabay sila, pare‑pareho ang linya?”

Show screenshots like the one from my own post so people can see the repetition with their own eyes.

Refuse to let “walang kaso” be the end of the conversation. Ask instead: why do cases against the powerful rarely move in the first place?

Most importantly, keep talking to the readers who are still thinking, still doubting, still trying to make sense of the mess.

The trolls — loud and soft — are not there to win an honest debate. They’re there to drain your energy and fog your vision.

Once you recognize that, you can choose which voices still deserve your time.

Ngayon panlang brutal ng presyo ng fuel and it will continue to rise sa mga susunod na linggo.

Starting March 10, diesel prices in Metro Manila jumped by P17.50 to P24.25 per liter. Gasoline surged by P7 to P13. Kerosene — the fuel of the poor, the fuel of fried fish and dimly lit sari-sari stores — went up by P32 to P38.50 per liter.

That was last week.

This week, March 17, the next wave is already coming. Industry sources told the Inquirer that diesel could rise by another P19.30 to P22.30 per liter, and gasoline by P14 to P17 per liter.

We are not at the peak. Not yet.

What's Driving This

It starts at the Strait of Hormuz. Around 20% of the world's oil passes through that narrow chokepoint between Iran and Oman. It's effectively closed right now following military actions linked to the US-Israel-Iran conflict.

The Philippines imports 98% of its crude oil from the Middle East. That is not a rounding error. That is almost complete dependence on a region that is, at this moment, on fire.

And because the Philippines uses the Mean of Platts Singapore (MOPS) as its pricing reference, whatever happens in the Gulf hits pump prices here almost immediately. There's no buffer. Energy Secretary Sharon Garin put it plainly: "Kung umakyat, talo tayo." When prices go up, we lose.

China made things worse. Beijing signaled it would restrict fuel exports to protect its own domestic supply, which forces the Philippines to compete for more expensive replacement cargoes from farther sources.

A Six-Week Projection (And Why It's Uncertain)

Here's the honest picture, week by week, based on what we know now:

Week 1 (March 17): Diesel up P19.30–P22.30/L, gasoline up P14–P17/L. This is already confirmed by industry sources. After last week's record hike, pump prices for diesel in North Luzon have already hit P70–P80/L, with premium diesel reaching P94.65/L.

Week 2–3 (March 24 – April 6): The trajectory points upward. Fitch raised its average 2026 oil price forecast to $70 per barrel, assuming the Strait of Hormuz stays effectively closed. MUFG Research warned that if oil sustains at $90–$100 per barrel, Philippine inflation could breach the BSP's 4% upper-end target for the entire year. More pump price hikes in the P10–P20 range per liter are highly likely in these two weeks if the Hormuz situation doesn't resolve.

Week 4–5 (April 13–26): This is the window where things could go either way. The DOE confirmed that local fuel inventories can last until the end of April. If supply diversification kicks in — the government is eyeing Brazil, Venezuela, the US, Canada, India, and Africa as alternative sources — pressure could ease slightly. The OPEC+ decision to add 206,000 barrels per day starting April 2026 is also a potential downward force.

Week 6 (April 27 – May 2): If the Hormuz closure persists into May, DEPDev's second scenario kicks in harder — $140/barrel crude, with diesel and gasoline prices staying above their current crisis levels through September. Under that scenario, there is no relief in Week 6.

The only wildcard working in consumers' favor: Marcos has certified as urgent a bill allowing him to suspend or reduce excise taxes on fuel. The House already approved it on second reading. If passed and invoked — diesel has a P6/L excise tax, gasoline has P10/L — that's a partial cushion, not a fix.

What It Means For Ordinary Filipinos

The LTFRB is already fielding active petitions for permanent fare hikes — P2 for jeepneys and city buses, 50 centavos per kilometer for provincial buses, and 30–40% increases for P2P services.

Public utility vehicles account for 65% of diesel consumption in the country. When diesel prices move, everything downstream moves — fares, freight, food.

Progressive groups have already used the phrase "inflation explosion." Meralco warned that electricity bills could go up by April. The peso closed at 59.735 on March 13, weakening under the weight of oil shock and war fear.

MUFG calculates that every $10/barrel increase in oil prices cuts Philippine GDP growth by around 0.2 percentage points and raises inflation by around 0.6 percentage points. We are not at $70/barrel right now. Dubai crude already breached $100 at the time of the latest major hike.

The DOE says don't panic buy. That is good advice.

But don't expect this to end soon.

He turned 58 last March 12.

I almost missed it. A few birthday posts from government offices, some warm messages from migrant workers' groups, and that was mostly it. No viral moment. No press conference. No grandiose statement about his own legacy.

That quiet, I think, is exactly the point.

Hans Leo Cacdac has spent the better part of three decades doing the unglamorous work.

Labor law. Conciliation boards. Recruitment agency inspections. OFW documentation.

The kinds of jobs that don't make front pages but make or break the lives of millions of Filipinos who pack their bags and leave to keep their families afloat.

I want to take a moment on that.

THE QUIET CONSISTENT ONE

When Cacdac was running the POEA under Aquino, civil society groups put out an affirmation of support for him. Not because he asked. Not because he had a communications team pushing it. Because migrant workers and CSOs felt seen enough to write it themselves.

That kind of thing doesn't happen by accident.

They noted that he took shifts sitting at the POEA information desk himself.

He personally responded to OFWs on social media.

He simplified documentation processes.

He cracked down on illegal recruiters without waiting for someone to tell him to.

The counter was never part of his job description, but he kept showing up there beside the staff.

That detail made me reflect.

There's something about a public servant who doesn't see the frontline as beneath them.

That kind of choice says more than any slogan.

BEFORE THE TITLE, THERE WAS THE WORK

When Susan Ople died in August 2023, there was a grief that went beyond the personal. She had built the DMW from the ground up. She had a name, a brand, a constituency. Her death left a hole that no one was going to easily fill.

Cacdac was named OIC the following month.

He didn't come in with a press tour. He came in with his track record. Decades at POEA, OWWA, DOLE. A law degree from Ateneo. A master's in comparative labor law from the United States. More importantly, years of knowing exactly what it costs a Filipino family when the system fails them abroad.

He was confirmed as full Secretary by the Commission on Appointments in August 2024, after an initial bypass, after oppositors, after all the bureaucratic friction that comes with actually being good at a job that some people don't want you in.

He got through it, and he went back to work.

THE MAN WHO INHERITED AN IMPOSSIBLE SEAT

In 2025, the DMW under Cacdac logged dozens of closure operations against illegal recruiters. There were convictions tied to scam hubs. Thousands of OFW beneficiaries were reached through assistance funds and legal support.

Those figures point to people who avoided trafficking, managed to protect their savings from fake agencies, and returned to their families alive.

When Filipinos were arrested in Doha in early 2025, the DMW had already begun moving even before most of us saw the headlines.

The response included visits to the detainees, lawyers on the ground, and steady coordination with the embassy team.

Provisional releases followed within days.

He stood at the podium and explained every step clearly, in Filipino, the way someone explains things to people they actually respect.

What shows up on camera comes from years of doing the work long before anyone was watching.

WHAT THE NUMBERS SAY

Kudos pieces rarely feel natural for me, and I tend to avoid them.

Praise in Philippine politics can feel premature, or worse, naive. We've seen enough people start well and end badly.

But there's a version of self-reflection I owe myself here.

I've spent a lot of time in this blog writing about what's wrong.

Between the trapos, the entrenched dynasties, and the larger systems that keep grinding ordinary people down, there's always something broken to write about. And all of that is real and worth writing about.

Pero minsan, you have to stop and name the exceptions.

Cacdac is a career public servant who came up through labor law and OFW advocacy before it was politically convenient.

He didn't arrive at the DMW as a political reward.

He arrived because he had been building toward it for thirty years.

His own father was an OFW. He didn't need to read a briefing to understand what the stakes are. He already knew.

That kind of formation is important.

WHY I'M WRITING THIS

We talk a lot about accountability in public service.

What I think we don't talk about enough is consistency.

The ability to show up the same way, at the information desk and at the Cabinet meeting.

To sit with migrant advocates, and on another day sign closure orders against recruiters who prey on the same workers you just listened to.

Cacdac has carried that pattern across different presidents and very different political climates.

His work has stretched across the Aquino, Duterte, and now Marcos administrations, carrying the same focus on OFWs through every change in Malacañang.

The politics around him changed, the alliances reshuffled, and yet his attention stayed on migrant workers and the systems that either protect or endanger them.

He has flaws, and the DMW still has plenty of gaps that need work. The institution is young, and the pressures on migrant workers keep evolving faster than our bureaucracy does.

There will be decisions to question and policies to challenge, and that’s part of the job of people like me who write about this.

In a government where consistency feels rare, I want to mark the moments when it actually shows up.

Happy birthday, Sec. Hans. Maraming salamat po sa consistent at walang kapagurang sakripisyo at dedikasyon sa trabaho para sa ating mga OFWs.

Mabuhay po kayo.
